evidence_quotes.quote: This kind of tactic is used prevalently in the video game industry that increasingly creates its products with an online-only function.
The game simply cannot be played offline.
The only thing that we as consumers demand is being able to access the content we've paid for after the official support has ended.

paragraphs.text: As a citizen of Germany, and therefore the EU, I'm quite content with most consumer rights that we have. That, however, does not seem to be the case regarding ownership of digital goods such as video games (or films). This also hampers artistic expression by game developers when a title that they had worked on with passion suddenly gets removed from the public eye - which it was created for. I remember distinctly playing a video game in the early 2010s named "BattleForge" which was a free-to-play strategy game supported by microtransactions. Yet, regardless of how much was spent, the game has been inaccessible since 2013 and an end-of-life plan was non-existent - meaning consumers no longer had any possible route to enjoy the purchases they had made throughout the game's lifespan. This kind of tactic is used prevalently in the video game industry that increasingly creates its products with an online-only function. This means that quite often a publisher decides to close down the game servers as soon as their product no longer turns enough profit - without, however, reimbursing their customers or allowing them to partake in the content they have paid for. The video game industry (among others) likes to hide behind words such as "licensing" its products - meanwhile the customer still "buys" or "purchases" whatever it is they have to offer. A product's description never entails that the game would be inaccessible in less than a year - which does not even match EU warranty laws. "Concord" - a game released in 2024 - was closed down in under a month after its release, despite having a several hundred million dollar budget. While the publisher (Sony Entertainment) did pay back all its customers, barely anyone had the opportunity to enjoy what had been created - which, by definition, is a piece of art. While that in itself is disappointing already, there are, at this point, hundreds of such cases where a previously purchased product is no longer usable despite being a digital good and often still installable through various launchers. Publishers like to say that they "cannot support a game indefinitely" and they're right about that; at the same time this phrase is no more than an excuse when there are ways to circumvent eternal financial support from the publisher themselves - a so-called "end-of-life plan". "Star Wars Galaxies" is a perfect example for that. Not only was this game online-only, it was an MMO - meaning that most game files are stored on a server instead of in the game client installed on a player's computer. The game simply cannot be played offline. It was shut down in 2011, yet is still playble to this day due to the developers releasing the files required to create a public server. No one is asking for these "online-only" games to be transformed into a single-player experience - which in many cases would be an almost impossible feat without requiring an absurd budget on top of the already high development costs. The only thing that we as consumers demand is being able to access the content we've paid for after the official support has ended. It is alright to have certain gameplay features missing as long as the base game is in a working state. As an EU-citizen I implore you to counteract these anti-consumer practices by demanding an end-of-life plan. Thank you.
